content.text: Capability Gap Analysis in Build vs Buy Decisions

A capability gap analysis maps the technical capabilities required to deliver a Claude solution against the capabilities your organization currently has. The gaps — requirements you cannot fulfill internally — determine how much risk a Build option carries and which specific capabilities you would need to acquire (through hiring, training, or purchasing a platform) before a build can succeed. Without this analysis, build decisions are optimistic estimates; with it, they are informed commitments.

attrs.alt: Capability Gap Analysis — Required vs Available quadrant diagram

content.text: Capability Domains for Claude Solutions

A Claude integration requires competence across six distinct capability domains. Teams that are strong in traditional software engineering often have significant gaps in the AI-specific domains, which are where build decisions most frequently fail.
Domain
Key Skills Required
Build Risk if Gap Exists
Prompt engineering
System prompt design, few-shot examples, chain-of-thought patterns, context management
Medium — learnable in 4–8 weeks with practice; plenty of public documentation
Evaluation and testing
Task-specific eval suite design, LLM-as-judge, golden output curation, regression harness
High — without evals you cannot measure or maintain quality; the gap is often discovered too late
LLM operations
Rate limit management, token budget tracking, retry logic, streaming, async handling
Low-Medium — well-documented in Anthropic SDK; most engineering teams adapt quickly
Security and compliance
Prompt injection defense, output filtering, data classification, SOC 2 evidence for AI systems
High — security reviews of AI systems require specialized knowledge; compliance gaps can block production deployment
Observability
LLM trace logging, cost tracking per request, quality monitoring in production, alert design
Medium — tooling exists (LangSmith, Helicone, custom logging) but requires platform decisions and ongoing maintenance
Domain expertise
Deep knowledge of the target domain (legal, medical, financial, etc.) to evaluate model outputs
High — without domain experts, you cannot author golden outputs, validate model responses, or design failure detection
Running the Capability Assessment

The capability gap analysis is structured as a team self-assessment across each domain. Each domain is rated on two axes: importance to the specific use case (high/medium/low) and current team capability (high/medium/low). The quadrant positions drive the build vs buy recommendation for each domain.

## Capability Assessment Template

Rate each domain on two dimensions:
- Importance: How critical is this capability to delivering the use case successfully?
  (H = failure without it, M = significant quality impact, L = minor friction)
- Team Capability: Does the team currently have this skill at production level?
  (H = multiple team members with production experience, M = some experience/adjacent skills,
   L = no experience or only theoretical knowledge)

| Domain               | Importance | Team Capability | Quadrant       | Action               |
|----------------------|------------|-----------------|----------------|----------------------|
| Prompt engineering   | H          | M               | Build gap      | Invest in 4-wk training sprint |
| Evaluation / testing | H          | L               | Critical gap   | Buy platform OR hire eval eng. |
| LLM operations       | M          | H               | Build          | Team owns this       |
| Security / compliance| H          | L               | Critical gap   | Buy platform OR engage specialist |
| Observability        | M          | L               | Buy/deprioritize| Use vendor tooling   |
| Domain expertise     | H          | H               | Build          | Domain team owns golden outputs |

Quadrant interpretation:
  High importance + Low capability = Critical gap → blocks Build; Buy or hire before committing
  High importance + High capability = Core competency → Build this
  Low importance + Low capability → Buy off the shelf; not worth building
  Low importance + High capability → Risk of over-engineering; resist the urge to Build
Critical Gap: Evaluation Capability

Of all the capability gaps that affect build vs buy decisions, the evaluation gap is the most dangerous because it is the most invisible. A team that ships a Claude integration without production-grade evals will not know it has a quality problem until users complain — by which time remediation is expensive and trust is damaged.
Signs of an Evaluation Gap
The team cannot articulate what a 'good' vs 'bad' model response looks like for the specific task.
There are no golden outputs or reference answers for the top 20 task types.
Quality is assessed manually by sampling 5–10 outputs before deployment and checking them by eye.
No regression tests run when the system prompt is changed.
The team discovered quality regressions from user feedback rather than from automated tests.
Closing the Evaluation Gap
Closing the evaluation gap before committing to Build requires:
Domain expert availability:

at least one person who can curate golden outputs for the target task. This is not an engineering role — it is a domain subject matter expert (SME) who understands what a correct model output looks like.
Eval engineering time:
at minimum 2–4 engineer-weeks to build the initial eval harness, score the first 100 tasks against golden outputs, and establish the CI regression gate.
Ongoing eval maintenance commitment:
the eval suite degrades as the use case evolves. Someone must own refreshing golden outputs and expanding the suite when new task types emerge. This is 0.1–0.2 FTE of ongoing work.

If your organization cannot name the person who will own the eval suite, you are not ready to Build. Buy a platform that includes quality monitoring, or delay the build decision until the ownership question is answered.
Critical Gap: Security and Compliance Capability

AI systems introduce security risks that standard application security reviews do not cover. Teams with strong traditional application security backgrounds often have significant gaps in AI-specific security assessment.
Traditional AppSec Skill
AI-Specific Equivalent
Gap Risk
SQL injection prevention
Prompt injection defense (user-controlled content in system prompt scope)
High — requires AI-specific security patterns, not traditional input sanitization
XSS prevention
Output filtering for downstream injection (generated HTML, code, etc.)
Medium — similar conceptual pattern but AI output is harder to predict and sanitize
Authentication / authorization
API key scoping, system prompt confidentiality, multi-tenant prompt isolation
Medium — similar patterns but new attack surfaces (prompt extraction, scope confusion)
Data classification
Classifying prompt content and model outputs for retention / privacy policy
High — traditional data classification systems were not designed for inference logs
Audit logging for compliance
LLM trace logging that captures input, output, tokens, model version for compliance evidence
Medium — tooling exists but requires integration choices not covered by standard SOC 2 control templates
Addressing the Security Gap
If your security team identifies critical gaps in AI security knowledge, the decision is:
Buy a platform with built-in AI security controls:

several enterprise Claude platforms (AWS Bedrock, Google Vertex AI, Azure OpenAI) include built-in guardrails, output filtering, and compliance logging that close the most common AI security gaps. The security team reviews the vendor's SOC 2 and compliance controls rather than building equivalent controls from scratch.
Engage an AI security specialist:

bring in a specialized AI security firm to conduct a pre-launch security assessment. Budget 2–4 weeks and $40–80k for a focused AI security assessment. This closes the knowledge gap for Build but requires internal security capacity to implement the findings.
Build with explicit constraints:

scope the Build to avoid the riskiest patterns. For example, avoid user-controlled content in the system prompt scope; use strict output validation; implement zero-retention mode. These constraints reduce the attack surface that requires AI-specific security expertise.
Gap Scoring and Decision Threshold

After completing the capability assessment, each gap can be scored to produce a quantitative build readiness score. This makes the recommendation defensible to stakeholders who want a structured basis for the decision.

# Build readiness scoring
DOMAINS = [
    "prompt_engineering",
    "evaluation_testing",
    "llm_operations",
    "security_compliance",
    "observability",
    "domain_expertise",
]

# Assessment input: importance (H=3, M=2, L=1) and team capability (H=3, M=2, L=1)
assessment = {
    "prompt_engineering":   {"importance": 3, "capability": 2},
    "evaluation_testing":   {"importance": 3, "capability": 1},  # critical gap
    "llm_operations":       {"importance": 2, "capability": 3},
    "security_compliance":  {"importance": 3, "capability": 1},  # critical gap
    "observability":        {"importance": 2, "capability": 1},
    "domain_expertise":     {"importance": 3, "capability": 3},
}

def build_readiness_score(assessment: dict) -> dict:
    critical_gaps = []
    weighted_score_total = 0
    max_score = 0

    for domain, scores in assessment.items():
        imp = scores["importance"]
        cap = scores["capability"]
        weighted_score_total += imp * cap
        max_score += imp * 3  # max capability is 3

        if imp == 3 and cap == 1:
            critical_gaps.append(domain)

    readiness_pct = (weighted_score_total / max_score) * 100

    return {
        "readiness_percent": round(readiness_pct, 1),
        "critical_gaps": critical_gaps,
        "recommendation": (
            "BUILD" if readiness_pct >= 70 and not critical_gaps else
            "BUY or close critical gaps first" if critical_gaps else
            "BUILD with targeted investments"
        ),
    }

result = build_readiness_score(assessment)
print(result)
# {'readiness_percent': 58.3, 'critical_gaps': ['evaluation_testing', 'security_compliance'],
#  'recommendation': 'BUY or close critical gaps first'}
Gap Closure Roadmap

When critical gaps exist but the organization still wants to Build, the gap closure roadmap specifies what must be done before the build can proceed. This is not about eliminating the Build option — it is about sequencing the investments so the build has a realistic chance of success.

## Gap Closure Roadmap — Pre-Build Requirements

Critical Gap 1: Evaluation Capability
  Owner: [AI Engineering Lead or designated SME]
  Timeline: 6 weeks before build starts
  Actions:
    - Week 1-2: Domain expert curates 100 golden outputs for top task types
    - Week 3-4: Engineer builds eval harness + scoring scripts
    - Week 5: First eval run against 3 model tiers; baseline established
    - Week 6: CI regression gate wired; eval suite ownership documented
  Exit criterion: eval harness runs cleanly against 100 tasks; CI gate blocks on quality drop

Critical Gap 2: Security / Compliance Capability
  Owner: [CISO / InfoSec Lead]
  Timeline: 4 weeks before build starts (can overlap with eval closure)
  Actions:
    - Week 1: Engage AI security specialist for scoped assessment (RFQ issued now)
    - Week 2-3: AI security review of proposed architecture + prompt design
    - Week 4: Implement required controls (prompt injection defenses, output filtering, audit logging)
  Exit criterion: AI security review sign-off; audit logging integrated; zero-retention confirmed

Build start: only after both exit criteria are met.
Estimated gap closure cost: $40k–$80k (security specialist) + 2 FTE-months (eval engineering)
This cost is part of the Build option TCO.
Capability Gap vs Knowledge Gap vs Capacity Gap
Not all gaps are the same. A capability gap analysis is most useful when it distinguishes between three types of shortfalls, because the remediation strategy differs for each.
Gap Type
Definition
Example
Remediation
Capability gap
The team lacks the specialized knowledge to do this well, even with unlimited time
No one on the team has designed an LLM eval suite before; they don't know what rubric scoring is
Training (4–8 weeks), hire, or Buy a platform that includes the capability
Knowledge gap
The team has the underlying skills but lacks specific domain or tooling knowledge
Engineers know how to build testing systems but haven't used Anthropic's SDK or LangSmith
Documentation review, pair programming, 1–2 week familiarization sprint
Capacity gap
The team has the capability but not enough time or people to do it within the deadline
The team has built eval systems before but is fully committed to other work for the next 6 months
Contract an SI, hire temporary staff, or extend the timeline

A capacity gap does not change the Build vs Buy recommendation by itself — it only changes the timeline and staffing plan. A capability gap, by contrast, means the team cannot achieve the required quality even if they had unlimited time. Capability gaps are the ones that disqualify the Build option without additional investment; capacity gaps are managed through planning.
Interviewing the Team: Assessment Anti-Patterns

Capability self-assessments are prone to systematic biases. Engineers tend to overestimate their capability in adjacent domains ("we built a recommendation system, so LLM evals should be similar") and underestimate how specialized AI-specific skills actually are. The assessment facilitator should probe for evidence rather than accepting self-reported confidence scores.
'We've used GPT before':

experience with one LLM does not transfer uniformly to Claude. Prompt engineering patterns, safety behaviors, and API ergonomics differ. Probe: 'Can you show me a Claude integration you've built and maintained in production?'
'We'll figure it out':
this response often signals a capacity gap disguised as confidence. Probe: 'What specifically would you need to learn, and how long would that take?'
'We have an AI/ML team':

ML training expertise (PyTorch, feature engineering, model training) does not map to LLM integration skills. The skill sets are largely orthogonal. Probe: 'Has anyone on that team built a production evaluation harness for an LLM-based system?'
'This is just a wrapper around an API':

the API call is the easy part. The hard parts are prompt engineering, evaluation, production monitoring, and graceful degradation. Teams that describe Claude integration as 'just an API call' are systematically underestimating the total capability requirement.
Capability Building vs Buying: The Hybrid Path

The Build vs Buy decision is not always binary. A common hybrid strategy is to Buy a platform initially — reducing time-to-production and closing critical gaps with vendor tooling — while simultaneously building internal capability through the integration process. After 6–12 months, the organization is equipped to evaluate whether to migrate to a Build approach or extend the Buy relationship.

## Hybrid Strategy: Buy → Build Transition Plan

Phase 1 (Month 1-3): Buy to unblock
- Deploy [Platform X] for the use case; integration takes 3-4 weeks
- Team operates the platform; closes prompt engineering knowledge gap through hands-on use
- Domain experts build golden outputs for the eval suite (vendor provides tooling)
- Security gap closed via vendor SOC 2 + DPA

Phase 2 (Month 4-9): Capability building alongside Buy
- Eval engineering team (1 FTE) builds internal eval harness in parallel
  (can run against the Buy platform's outputs to validate)
- Security team completes AI security assessment training
- Domain team codifies golden output rubrics in version control

Phase 3 (Month 10-12): Decision point
- Run Build vs Buy TCO update with 9 months of actual volume data
- Run build readiness score with updated capability assessment
- If readiness ≥ 70% and TCO favors Build at current+projected volume: migrate
- If not: extend Buy contract for Year 2; continue capability building

Outcome: Either decision at Month 12 is informed and reversible.
The worst outcome (locked into Buy with no capability) is avoided.

The hybrid approach costs slightly more in Year 1 than a pure Buy (you are paying for the platform AND investing in internal capability). It is worth the extra cost if your long-term trajectory is toward high volume or deep customization that will eventually require Build.
Summary

A capability gap analysis maps required Claude solution skills — prompt engineering, evaluation, LLM operations, security/compliance, observability, and domain expertise — against current team capability on a 2×2 importance vs capability matrix. Critical gaps are capabilities that are both high-importance and low-capability; these block the Build option until closed. The two most common critical gaps in enterprise Claude build decisions are evaluation capability (no golden outputs, no regression harness, no quality measurement) and AI security capability (prompt injection defense, AI-specific audit logging, output filtering). A quantitative readiness score (weighted importance × capability across domains) provides a defensible threshold for recommending Build vs Buy. When critical gaps exist but Build is still preferred, a gap closure roadmap specifies the prerequisite investments with timelines and exit criteria before the build engineering sprint begins.
